title: Allocation Formulas, Overflow, and Capacity Proofs · C Memory Workbench
summary: A guided implementation pair receives a clean input trace and acceptance examples, then builds the first working overflow-safe grid allocator while narrating each size, lifetime, ownership, and cleanup decision. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of overflow-safe grid allocator while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Prove count times element-size representability before calling allocation functions.
Check header, padding, alignment, terminator, and payload additions in a safe order.
Characterize whether the target calloc detects product overflow while retaining caller-side policy and tests.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Multiply Before Allocation
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop native-systems team handles memory case 3806. Produce bounded C evidence to prove a header-plus-payload allocation formula; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. Instant code stays inside the PicoC practice subset. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Multiply Before Allocation.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for prove count times element-size representability before calling allocation functions. options.text: For test an acceptance boundary, run one convenient Capacity Model Proof case, omit size, bounds, lifetime, owner, allocation failure, cleanup, invariant output, and native transfer gates, then call the browser result production-safe. Keep the plausible Capacity Model Proof output and hide the violated overflow-safe grid allocator assumption instead of explaining the failure. That claim fails the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary for Capacity Model Proof.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model · Workshop Capacity Model Proof
steps.instruction:
Write the workshop evidence note for Capacity Model Proof: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A workshop native-systems team handles memory case 9377. Produce bounded C evidence to prove a header-plus-payload allocation formula; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. Instant code stays inside the PicoC practice subset. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Derive maximum capacity from object limit, element size, metadata, and stakeholder workload before allocation.

steps.why: Retrieving Capacity Model Proof through the concrete overflow-safe grid allocator case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Defend the transferred model · Workshop Capacity Model Proof, keep Capacity Model Proof connected to executable C memory invariant and explicit native transfer evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Derive maximum capacity from object limit, element size, metadata, and stakeholder workload before allocation.
